data.description:
Motio Research's initial estimate of U.S. median household income in October 2024 is $82,222. This figure is $85 (0.1%) less than the firm's initial estimate of median household income in September 2024 of $82,307.

data.title: Donville Kent Asset Management November 2024 Commentary
data.description:
It has become a common opening comment for us to say that the overall thesis on declining rates, cheap small cap valuations, and cash on the sidelines continues to support strong returns. The fund was up 9.36%1 in October bringing the year-to-date return to 83.88%1.

In this monthly update we will provide a brief update on these three investment tailwinds, but we will spend most of the time speaking to specific investments as we just completed a busy Q3 earnings season. Near the end of this commentary, we provide some more detailed discussion on specific investment valuations but also an overview of a general framework we use.

In Mid-October we hosted an investor Webinar where we discussed our market outlook, top picks, and answered questions. Please reach out if you would like the link to download the video.

Three Main Tailwinds

Inflation & Declining Rates

We recently used the graph below to illustrate how important the direction of rates is on stock market returns. The top line is market returns when the market is advancing and rates are falling versus when the market is advancing and rates are rising.

Figure 1 - Stock Market Performance

We can lump many countries into the same scenario, but in Canada especially, rates have peaked and have begun to fall. The most common pushback we’ll get at the moment is possible renewed inflation based on increasing tariffs and government spending. Our main point we’re trying to make here is that there are stronger forces at play than policy decisions.

Tariffs were placed on China during Trump’s last presidency and prices for goods out of China decreased.

Government spending per capita has increased significantly since the 70’s & 80’s but inflation declined (Covid aside).

There are more important forces at play that have led to interest rates peaking in 1980 and declining since then.

The stronger forces at play are demographics and increased productivity. If there are fewer people, there’s less demand for goods. If productivity increases, we can supply more goods at less cost. Both are deflationary. We’ve used a different chart in the past, but we came across a new one that backs up this point. We argue much of the inflation in the 70’s & 80’s was due to a massive growth in the population post WWII. The birth rate per family is now plummeting for most of the world. China’s population is actually declining year over year and based on these trends, many countries around the world will see similar declines over the coming decades. Productivity continues to improve. We’ve gone through the industrial revolution, the invention of the internet and are now deploying AI technology.

Figure 2 - Population Growth by Decade

Figure 3 - Productivity per Hour Worked

All this said, we think the declining rate environment is sustainable and supportive for investment returns.

Valuations

Valuations in the market in general are not cheap, however the delta between large caps and small caps is still near all-time highs. We're much more positive on small caps going forward than large caps.

Figure 4 - Small vs Large Caps

A quick point of emphasis – This does not mean owning small cap indexes is the best way to gain small cap exposure, which we spoke to HERE.

Cash on the Sidelines

Here is an updated chart of cash in US money market funds as of November 13th (with previous tops highlighted).

Figure 5 - Money Market Assets. From Bloomberg

As rates decline, alternatives to cash become more attractive, similar to 2000-2002 and 2008-2009. Trillions of cash comes off the sidelines and back into the market. As cycles do what cycles do, new capital should enter the market and the gap in large cap versus small cap should more than narrow.

Election Results

We’ll briefly touch on the new administration with some high-level points, but it is obviously still early. We think:

  • The $CAD will be weak as Canada lowers rates at a faster pace than the US
    • This makes Canadian investments significantly cheaper and attractive for US based investors or potential acquirers
  • Certain industries like solar, security, EVs, biotech, tariff & retaliation tariff related (especially China imports), will have more uncertainty making them harder to value
    • Our fund's potential tariff exposure is minimal
  • Less regulation is good for technology, financials, and energy sectors
  • Small caps particularly benefit as they are more domestically focused
  • Lower taxes, deregulation, and improving productivity all boost earnings, and the direction of earnings is the best indicator of future returns
  • A lot of uncertainty around budget deficits and geopolitical tensions

Q3 Earnings Summaries

We just went through Q3 earnings season, and we'll highlight many of the results below. The two main points to reinforce are:

  1. Results have been strong. In most cases growth has been even better than anticipated and profit margins continue to improve
  2. Stocks have bounced back considerably since 2022. BUT, looking at investment by investment, many have positioned themselves to have stellar 2025s. We're excited to see the market price in these inflection points.

Constellation Software (CSU:CA, OTCPK:CNSWF)

Revenue $2.5B +20%

Cash Earnings $506M +20% 2

Cash Margin 20% 2

Spent $197M on acquisitions in the quarter

Spent $359M on acquisitions post-quarter

EBITDA beat with higher than expected margins

M&A after the quarter has been strong

Stock is now trading on historical low end of forward PE

Constellation Software announced a fairly stable quarter. We’ve received a few questions regarding the impact of AI on vertical market software. CSU management addressed the question at their most recent AGM, “Constellation has some businesses that generate meaningful revenue from AI. Perseus has been developing AI since the 2010s.” Management sees this like another tech shift, similar to mobile, and will adapt like they have in the past. From our perspective, we always start with growth and profit margins. If there are issues in a business model, they will surface in growth and profitability. CSU just reported 6% organic growth in its main business with increasing margins (partly due to AI efficiency). I know that is today and doesn’t address the future, and we’re well aware and looking into new AI applications almost daily.

VitalHub (VHI:CA, OTCQX:VHIBF)

ARR $53.5M +25%

Revenue $16.5M +25%

Adj. EBITDA $4.6M +33%

EBITDA Margin 28%

Cash Earnings $3.3M 3

Cash Margin 20%

After the quarter they closed MedCurrent and Strata acquisitions

These acquisitions grow ARR by 27%

After factoring in these acquisitions, they have over $50M of cash and no debt

When asked if there will be a pause in M&A after these two deals

“There’s still stuff in play”

“If there’s a good deal that makes sense, we’ll get it done”

VitalHub reported a very strong quarter with FCF/Share growing 31% YoY. Importantly, their two most recent acquisitions appear to be growing quickly. Profit margins will be impacted in the short term but the ability to integrate well is now a staple at VHI. The stock is no longer "cheap" using current estimates, but they are still sitting on a relatively large amount of cash with no debt. Future acquisitions are not factored into future growth estimates, the company consistently grows +20%, and they have +20% cash earnings margins...where have we seen this story playout before?!

VerticalScope (FORA:CA, OTCQX:VFORF)

Revenue $17.8M +15%

Monthly Active Users 122M +21%

Record number of users

Cash Earnings $5.6M +28% 4

Cash Margin 32% 4

Paid back $5.6M in debt

Roughly 1.2x Debt/EBITDA

Bought back $2.5M shares YTD

Closing a handful of tuck-ins in Q4

More inbound activity

Better multiples than they have seen the last 2 years

Closed automotive OEM advertisers for Q4

Expect low double digit organic growth for 2025

VerticalScope has reached an inflection point. Their organic growth has returned, they have reduced debt levels significantly, and they are starting to complete tuck-ins. FORA is one of the cheapest stocks we follow just based on the current business, but when they eventually announce licensing agreements with LLMs the stock will no longer be ignored. The division of Nordstar assets after arbitration in 2023, left Tevir Capital with ~3.3m shares. Based on recent filings plus a rumoured large block trade, it appears that this shareholder is exiting FORA. For a stock without a ton of daily liquidity, having a 3.3m share overhang can be significant.

Cipher Pharmaceuticals (CPH:CA, OTCQX:CPHRF)

Revenue $10.4M +71%

Gross Profit $8.4M +68%

Adj. EBITDA $4.1M

EBITDA Margin 39%

Cash Earnings $2.2M 4, 5

Cash Margin 21%

Over $3M in costs associated with closing the Natroba acquisition

Cash earnings would have been $5.2M +56%

Cipher reported a quarter that included 2 months of their recent Natroba acquisition. We were impressed with the quarter after adjusting for only two months of Natroba and adjusting for the extra costs associated with closing the deal. There was some duplication of costs in the quarter as they hired 7 salespeople to take over a wholesaler agreement which ends at the end of 2024. We believe this is what led to some of the weakness in Absorbica sales as well. 2025 should be a great year for Cipher as Natroba growth accelerates in the US with improved marketing, they acquire complimentary products, move ahead with Health Canada approval for Natroba, as well as advance rest of the world licensing deals.

GoEasy (GSY:CA, OTCPK:EHMEF)

Loan Originations $839M +16%

Loan Portfolio $4.39B +28%

Allowance for Credit Losses 9.2% vs 8.5% YoY

Revenue $383M +19%

Operating Income $160M +26%

Cash Earnings $91M +26% 6

Net Margin 24%

EPS $4.88 +26%

ROE 29%

Repurchased $9M of shares since quarter-end

“On track to achieve all forecasted metrics for 2024”

GoEasy reported a strong quarter, but the stock has corrected since then. We’re not worried about the underlying business performance, but the stock does have headline risk as investors assume a weak Canadian economy implies a weak operating environment for GSY. We usually use dips in this stock as buying opportunities.

Kneat (KSI:CA)(OTCQX:KSIOF)

Revenue $12.8M +52%

SaaS Revenue $11.5M +48%

ARR $50M +59%

Gross Profit $9.8M +78%

Gross Margin 77%

Adj. EBITDA $2.9M (2 nd quarter of positive EBITDA)

EBITDA Margin 23%

Cash Earnings $1.1M 6

Cash Margin 9% 6

Kneat is now reaching a scale where earnings ability is starting to shine through. This is the second quarter in a row of accelerated year-over-year growth in both revenue and gross profit. The stock is currently hitting new highs, and they continue to announce new deals which support significant growth for multiple years.

Propel (PRL:CA)

Revenue $117M +41%

Originations $150M +36%

Loan Balance $432M +44%

Net Charge Offs 11% vs. 12% in Q2/24

Adj. EBITDA $29M +56%

EBITDA Margin 25%

Cash Earnings $12.4M +70% 7

Cash Margin 11%

ROE 45% (compared to 27% in Q2/24)

Increased dividend by 7%, yields 1.62%

QuidMarket acquisition closed ahead of expectations

Officially launched partnership with Koho in Canada

Expect margins to continue to expand with scale

Foresee significant growth ahead in US, Canada, and UK

Propel’s quarter was strong as they had both strong growth and strong margins. The summary above doesn’t include the acquisition of QuidMarket which closed mid-November. The stock has performed well and we expect performance to continue as the current valuation is attractive in relation to future growth in earnings, especially after factoring in the QuidMarket accretion.

MDA Space (MDA:CA) (OTCPK:MDALF)

Revenue $282M +38%

Backlog $4.6B +49%

Gross Profit $76M +31%

Adj. EBITDA $56M +30%

EBITDA Margin 20%

Cash Earnings $37M +72% 7

Cash Margin 13% 7

Debt/EBITDA 0.8x

Slightly raised revenue and EBITDA guidance for 2025

Implies 30% revenue growth with 20% EBITDA margins

Current pipeline of discussions is $15B

Expected backlog to be $5B by end of 2024

Will begin to look more at M&A in Q4 and Q1

Looking to secure supply chain

MDA Space has been checking all the boxes. They have reduced debt, increased their backlog, expanded manufacturing capacity, while growth has accelerated, and margins improved. The growth in the space industry, specifically satellites, has been impressive. There continues to be new technology companies entering the sector, plus significant upgrade cycles ahead (hence the $15B in pipeline discussions).

Enterprise and Zedcor

Before we jump into the quarterly results of Enterprise and Zedcor, we want to highlight our recent keynote presentation from the Planet MicroCap Showcase in Vancouver. The full video has been uploaded to YouTube and can be found HERE. The presentation focuses on identifying the characteristics of a true compounding company. We give past, present and future examples, which are Enterprise Group and Zedcor.

Enterprise Group (E:CA)

Enterprise’s Q3 was hindered by forest fires in Western Canada, and we have used the pullback after the release as a buying opportunity. Similar to the playbook on Zedcor, Enterprise is proving out a new technology in the natural gas sector, but it has applications in other large sectors like mining and data centers. We think Enterprise is positioning themselves to be a long-term player in servicing the secular demand for reliable cheap power in North America.

Using their turbine technology is significantly superior to the conventional diesel engines:

Cost savings to the customer are +86% Emissions reduction of 85-100% Diesel generators are loud plus need to be serviced every ~600 hours Natural gas turbines have maintenance intervals in the +10,000 hours Their turbines are extremely fuel tolerant and they just announced successful implementation of using flare gas to power the turbines and supply energy back to the client’s grid

Figure 6 - 350kW Diesel Generator vs 333kW Gas Turbine

Figure 7 - 350kW Diesel Generator vs 333kW Gas Turbine

We highlighted Enterprise as a compounder because turbines are extremely profitable with +40% IRR and 20-25-year lifetimes. This allows Enterprise to take their cashflow and reinvest it back into the business at high rates of return for a long time. There are multiple hundreds of natural gas sites in Canada that they see as the low hanging fruit and significant capital is being invested in this sector as multiple new LNG pipelines are set to come online which allows Canada to ship natural outside of North America for the first time. Plus, this technology is applicable to mining and data centers. As the power division scales, now representing 45% of the business, the business will re-rate as a power/grid company versus an energy services business. We’ll delve more into this opportunity and provide a deeper dive into this investment in a future piece.

Zedcor (ZDC:CA, OTCPK:CRFQF)

Revenue $9.2M +42%

Adj. EBITDA $3.4M +49%

EBITDA Margin 36%

Cash Margin 14% 8

Total fleet utilization of close to 100% in the quarter

Deployed 148 towers in the quarter

Total fleet 1,151 towers (expect +3,000 in a year)

Hired 2 enterprise-level sales teams

Now expanding into Colorado, Arizona, & Midwest

The US now represents 10-20% of total business

Weekly tower production continues to improve

25 per week by end of year (at 11.4/week now)

Just expanded their facility which increases capacity to 30-50 towers/week

Nov 12 th = 1,296 fleet

50 Z Box units (new product line)

Sales team in the US now 12 people (was 2 people in June)

Expect to launch 2 new locations in Canada in 2025

“In first inning in Canada and just getting out of the dugout in US”

Zedcor reported a great Q3. We’ve described this investment in detail in recent commentaries and newsletters, so we’ll focus on the recent progress. They are on track to be producing 25 towers per week by the end of the year with the goal to be able to produce +50 towers/week next year, which is ~2,600/year. Profit margins were impressive in the quarter and management noted that as they scale, they will bring costs of manufacturing down further plus inherent efficiencies as new locations mature. On the call they noted that locations like Toronto are much higher margin than the rest of the business due to scale.

Of note are their two recent hires who will focus on winning enterprise clients. They hired the Senior VP of Security at Walmart/Home Depot plus they hired the former Chief of Security at Trans Mountain Pipeline & Assistant Commissioner of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. These new hires and ongoing discussions with the likes of Amazon, Walmart, BestBuy, and some of the largest home builders, we expect announcements around chunky orders in the 300-500 tower range that can get them closer to 10,000 towers over the next ~3-4 years which is roughly 10x the size they are now.

This leads to the popular comment when a stock like this has performed so well.“It’s expensive.” In order to respond, we’ll go through a helpful exercise which we do for all our investments.

Zedcor is very much a price times quantity business. Fairly easy to model if you’re on top of the demand and supply dynamics. We respect and agree with Cormark's projected outlook of getting to +7,000 towers by 2028 without factoring in large enterprise client wins. Our own work suggests they will blow through their 2025 projections and utilization rates will remain between 90-100%. As described, if they match their new manufacturing capacity with growing salesforce, then getting to 7,000 towers by the end of 2027 should be the low-end projection and doesn’t factor in enterprise wins. Under this scenario, one can apply a utilization rate and profit margin to come to projected earnings. Next apply an earnings multiple, and you’ve now calculated your future investment return under that scenario.

Conservatively, using 7,000 towers, an 85% utilization rate, lower profit margins than anticipated and 10x multiple equals an annualized return on the stock of 24%. Not bad and definitely not expensive.

Trying to be more accurate, if overall profit margins continue to improve toward their established location margins, they have success with enterprise and get to 10,000 towers, plus trade on the industry multiple (15x), the annualized return on the stock would be 53% (+450% aggregate).

A way to double check this math is to compare future returns with per tower IRR, which right now we believe is +40% and should improve with scale. Putting multiple aside, future growth and stock returns should mirror the company’s ability to deploy capital, so a +40% IRR leading to a +40% annualized return in the stock over time is not incomprehensible.

A 53% annualized return does not agree with the statement that the stock is expensive. A lot of people strictly look at earnings multiples. A multiple in isolation isn’t useful. Similar to plainly saying a house that cost $400k is cheap and one that is $1m is expensive. In reality, a stock trading on 10x earnings can be expensive and a stock trading on 20x earnings can be cheap. One needs to factor in growth (compound growth), margins, ROE etc., to see if the multiple they are paying today is worth owning the company or not.

This is an extremely useful exercise. Think where the company should be in 3-4 years. What type of profits will they generate in that scenario? Apply a fair but conservative earnings multiple and see if the annualized return is worth your investment. There are a lot of stocks out there right now, mostly large caps, where the future returns aren’t that appealing using this exercise.

A fun exercise where a curious investor could use this methodology to see how the math can go against you, is on companies like WingStop or Cava. Estimate how many locations they will have in 3-4 years, apply a profit margin and earnings multiple and see what kind of return that implies. It most likely won’t be a positive one!

If we haven’t mentioned an investment, it is because we are either in the process of trimming/selling, have sold the position or are restricted on the name.

Final Thoughts

It is an interesting market right now. The Canadian economy leaves a lot to be desired. The US is fairly strong, but will have some volatility with the new administration. BUT, we are excited for 2025 simply based on looking at our largest investments and how well they have positioned themselves for next year. Many have been executing strategies where their growth inflects over the next 12-24 months.

Obvious risks going forward are policy risks (tariffs, renewed inflation, taxes), and a continued slowing of the Canadian economy plus global economic weakness, especially in Europe and China. Having a concentrated portfolio of investments, one should be able limit exposure to many of these risks and focus on areas either insulated from most outside forces or riding a secular tailwind.

Please sign-up for our distribution list on our website to receive monthly commentaries and quarterly newsletters. As always, please feel free to reach out with questions or comments.

J.P. Donville & Jesse Gamble

Footnotes

1 Time Weighted Rate of Return as of October 31, 2024, net of fees and expenses.

2 DKAM Cash earnings takes net earnings and adds back certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

3 DKAM Cash earnings takes net earnings and adds back certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

4 DKAM Cash earnings takes net earnings and adds back certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

5 Assuming costs with closing Natroba deal plus Natroba expenses

6 Cash earnings is taking net earnings and adding certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

7 DKAM Cash earnings takes net earnings and adds back certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

8 DKAM Cash earnings takes net earnings and adds back certain non-cash items such as amortization of intangible assets

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
